ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mushroom Tikka; closest defensible anchor is 'mushroom food product' (FOODON_03301710). Tikka preparation specifics are not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Mushrooms, prepared) is the closest defensible match. No specific tikka or tandoor-grilled mushroom code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Mushroom Tikka as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Mushroom Tikka is one plate of approximately 6–8 skewered mushroom pieces, weighing around 200 g, typically served as an appetizer or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Mushroom Tikka are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for button mushrooms, yogurt (hung curd), and spices. No exact Mushroom Tikka entry exists in ICMR-NIN; values represent a recipe-level estimate consistent with the described preparation. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values from the input were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for yogurt and mushrooms and are retained as plausible estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.) and University of Sydney GI Database — mushroom and yogurt analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mushroom Tikka. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for button mushrooms (GI ~10–15, negligible carbohydrate) and plain yogurt (GI ~36). The combined dish has low net carbohydrate (6 g/100 g) and the protein and fat from yogurt and oil further attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (25 × 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 3.0 g. This is a defensible low-GI, low-GL estimate consistent with ICMR-NIN guidance on mushroom-based dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (325 mg/100 g) from marinade salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt in the marinade, as a 200 g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, which represents a meaningful fraction of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low net carbohydrate (6 g/100 g) with high protein (10 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mushroom Tikka is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index and low glycemic load (GL ~3 per 200 g serving); the high protein content from yogurt marinade further blunts postprandial glucose response, making it a clinically appropriate snack or appetizer when consumed in standard portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (7.5 g/100 g) from hung curd and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contribution (approximately 15 g per 200 g serving), which approaches the upper daily limit of 20 g recommended by cardiovascular guidelines; choosing low-fat yogurt for the marinade and minimizing added oil can reduce saturated fat by an estimated 30–40%.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant compounds in mushrooms (selenium, ergothioneine) and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, garam masala)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mushroom Tikka may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of mushroom selenium and spice compounds; no specific dietary restriction applies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom Tikka is a popular North Indian vegetarian appetizer and snack in which plump button mushrooms are marinated in a spiced hung curd (strained yogurt) mixture containing ginger-garlic paste, carom seeds (ajwain), red chili powder, and garam masala, then skewered with bell peppers and onions and grilled in a tandoor oven or over a charcoal grill until tender with a characteristic smoky char. The dish delivers a rich, smoky, mildly spiced flavour with a juicy, slightly firm texture from the mushrooms and a tangy note from the yogurt marinade. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 190 kcal, 10 g protein, 14 g fat (7.5 g saturated), and 6 g carbohydrate with 1.5 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy, high-protein snack. The low glycemic index (~25) and low glycemic load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content (325 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. Mushrooms contribute selenium, potassium, riboflavin, and niacin, while the yogurt marinade adds calcium and vitamin B12. Mushroom Tikka is a widely served alternative to Paneer Tikka for those who prefer fungi-based dishes, and is commonly found on Indian restaurant menus across India, Singapore, the UK, and the Indian diaspora globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mushroom Tikka?
Is Mushroom Tikka good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Mushroom Tikka and Paneer Tikka?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for button mushrooms and yogurt; no exact Mushroom Tikka entry exists in any approved primary source. Values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference foods. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Mushroom Tikka nutrient profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mushroom Tikka (marinated and grilled preparation) are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the grilled composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is retained at 0.25 g/100 g as a plausible trace value from the yogurt and oil components, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for dairy-containing preparations. Cholesterol (35 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g) originate from the hung curd (yogurt) component and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for plain whole-milk yogurt. The input listed three duplicate portion entries for '1 serving (150g)'; only one unique portion per label is retained in this record. Portions sourced from 'grabfood' in the input have been retained as contextual reference only and are not used as primary nutrient evidence per Project Veritas source policy.
